diseases.name: Atrial Fibrillation
diseases.geneticRiskDescription: Your heart has a genetic tendency to follow an irregular rhythm. Your risk is above the average for this disease, but here's the exciting part: this is only the starting line, not the finish line!
diseases.activationDescription: Right now these 'beat-skipping' genes are notably awake (54% active), elevated by jet lag-induced autonomic stress and sleep deprivation. Restoring sleep is the fastest lever to quiet them.

diseases.name: Coronary Artery Disease
diseases.geneticRiskDescription: Genetic markers indicate a high risk for plaque accumulation. However, epigenetics can override this via high-quality fats and antioxidant-rich diet.
diseases.activationDescription: Activation elevated from baseline. Cortisol from travel stress and poor sleep is amplifying lipid metabolism gene expression. Hydration and rest will help.

diseases.name: Hypertension
diseases.geneticRiskDescription: Elevated genetic risk for salt sensitivity. Your body holds onto sodium more than most.
diseases.activationDescription: Significantly elevated. Dehydration from long-haul travel and elevated cortisol are directly activating salt-retention pathways. Rehydrating aggressively is critical.

diseases.name: Heart Failure
diseases.geneticRiskDescription: Your genetic predisposition for structural heart weakness is above average. Consistent aerobic exercise is critical.
diseases.activationDescription: Moderately elevated. Reduced exercise volume this week and elevated resting HR are nudging these pathways upward. Returning to routine will reverse this quickly.

diseases.name: Peripheral Artery Disease
diseases.geneticRiskDescription: Above average risk for peripheral circulatory issues. Your genetic baseline requires attentive lifestyle management.
diseases.activationDescription: Elevated by prolonged sitting during long-haul flight and reduced daily movement. Getting steps in will directly cool these pathways.

diseases.name: Type 2 Diabetes
diseases.geneticRiskDescription: High genetic baseline for insulin resistance. Dietary and lifestyle optimization are critical to prevent manifestation.
diseases.activationDescription: Jet lag disrupts circadian insulin secretion, spiking this activation above your normal baseline. Avoiding late-night eating and restoring sleep rhythm is essential.

diseases.name: Genetic Obesity
diseases.geneticRiskDescription: Your genes for satiety and fat storage are above population average. Structured caloric strategy is key.
diseases.activationDescription: Elevated. Disrupted circadian rhythm suppresses leptin (satiety hormone) and increases ghrelin (hunger). Travel snacking compounds this effect.

diseases.name: Liver Steatosis
diseases.geneticRiskDescription: Elevated genetic risk for non-alcoholic liver fat accumulation. High-fiber diet is essential.
diseases.activationDescription: Moderately elevated. Erratic meal timing during travel and airport food choices are stressing these metabolic pathways. Returning to routine diet will help.

diseases.name: Metabolic Syndrome
diseases.geneticRiskDescription: High genetic risk for clustering of metabolic conditions. Consistent monitoring is essential.
diseases.activationDescription: Elevated across-the-board metabolic stress from jet lag is pushing this into high territory. Recovery week is the priority.

diseases.name: PCOS Risk
diseases.geneticRiskDescription: Above average genetic risk for hormonal metabolic imbalances. Regular hormonal screening recommended.
diseases.activationDescription: Moderately elevated. Cortisol from travel stress is mildly activating hormonal imbalance pathways.

diseases.name: Alzheimer's Disease
diseases.geneticRiskDescription: You carry strong markers that correlate with faster cognitive decline if left unmanaged.
diseases.activationDescription: Significantly elevated. Sleep deprivation is the single biggest activator of APOE-ε4 risk — amyloid clearance only happens during deep sleep. Five nights of disrupted sleep has measurably spiked this pathway.

diseases.name: Parkinson's Disease
diseases.geneticRiskDescription: Genetic risk for dopaminergic neuron degeneration is above average. Antioxidant-rich nutrition is vital.
diseases.activationDescription: Elevated. Travel-related oxidative stress and circadian misalignment are known LRRK2 pathway triggers. Prioritise antioxidant foods and sleep.

diseases.name: Multiple Sclerosis
diseases.geneticRiskDescription: Above average genetic risk for neuro-immune conditions. Active monitoring recommended.
diseases.activationDescription: Moderately elevated. Immune function is suppressed from poor sleep, which can activate these neuro-immune markers.

diseases.name: Ischemic Stroke
diseases.geneticRiskDescription: High genetic tendency for vascular-neuro events. Strong interaction with blood pressure markers.
diseases.activationDescription: Elevated. Dehydration from travel raises blood viscosity, directly interacting with this genetic variant. Rehydrate urgently.

diseases.name: Chronic Migraine
diseases.geneticRiskDescription: Highly sensitive genetic neurological profile. Your brain reacts strongly to environmental triggers.
diseases.activationDescription: Very high. Jet lag is a clinically documented TRPM8 trigger — circadian disruption, dehydration, and barometric pressure change from Sofia (550m) to SF (52m) are all firing this pathway simultaneously.

diseases.name: Breast Cancer
diseases.geneticRiskDescription: Elevated genetic risk in cell repair and tumor suppression pathways. Annual screening strongly recommended.
diseases.activationDescription: Mildly elevated. Sleep deprivation reduces DNA repair efficiency, which nudges these pathways upward temporarily.

diseases.name: Prostate Cancer
diseases.geneticRiskDescription: Genetic indicators for oncological risk are above population average. PSA monitoring warranted.
diseases.activationDescription: Moderately elevated. Chronic stress and cortisol elevation from travel are mildly activating these oncological pathways.

diseases.name: Colorectal Cancer
diseases.geneticRiskDescription: Elevated genetic risk for gut mucosal compromise. High-fiber diet is a strong epigenetic lever.
diseases.activationDescription: Mildly elevated. Airport food and disrupted gut microbiome rhythm from travel are activating these markers.

diseases.name: Melanoma
diseases.geneticRiskDescription: High genetic sensitivity to UV radiation and DNA damage repair speed. Sunscreen is non-negotiable.
diseases.activationDescription: Elevated. SF's UV index (7) is meaningfully higher than Sofia's (4) in April. Your MC1R sensitivity makes this transition particularly relevant — SPF 30+ daily.

diseases.name: Lung Cancer
diseases.geneticRiskDescription: Genetic risk for pulmonary cell mutation is low. High detoxification resilience.
diseases.activationDescription: Low. SF air quality is good (AQI 40). No concern.

uv.recommendation: SPF 30+, wear a hat during midday exposure
